slides.heading: AI Terminology Decoded
slides.terms: AI · LLM · Token · Prompt · Hallucination · Agent · RAG · Tool · Skills · MCP · API · CLI
slides.subtitle: What do the terms mean, how do they fit together, and what are the most common misconceptions?

slides.title: AI
slides.subtitle: Artificial Intelligence
description: Artificial intelligence is the broad research field focused on getting machines to perform tasks that normally require human intelligence — recognition, decision-making, language understanding, planning.

AI is the umbrella. Beneath it sit machine learning, deep learning, LLMs, computer vision, robotics, and much more. When people say "AI" today, they usually mean LLM-based products — but the field is far broader.

callout.label: Common confusion
callout.title: "AI can think and feel"
callout.body: AI processes patterns in data — it doesn't think or feel. It's an advanced tool that can simulate understanding, but it has no consciousness, intentions, or desires.

example.scenario: AI is already in your pocket — long before chatbots existed.

blocks.label: Email
blocks.content: Spam filtering automatically sorts unwanted mail from real ones.

blocks.label: Phone
blocks.content: Face unlock recognizes you in a fraction of a second.

blocks.label: Maps
blocks.content: Real-time routing predicts traffic from millions of trips.

example.takeaway: All of these are AI — none of them are LLMs.

slides.title: LLM
slides.subtitle: Large Language Model
description: A large language model is a type of AI model trained on enormous amounts of text to understand and generate language. It predicts the most likely next word based on the context.
LLMs like GPT-4, Claude, and Llama are the "engine" behind modern AI assistants. They can write, analyze, translate, and reason — but they're still just one tool in the AI toolbox.

callout.label: Common misconception
callout.title: "LLM = AI"
callout.body: An LLM is one type of AI — like a gasoline engine is one type of engine. AI is the umbrella covering everything from image recognition to robotics. An LLM specializes in language.

example.scenario: Predicting the next word, again and again — until the answer is complete.

blocks.label: User input
blocks.content: "Write a polite reply declining tomorrow's client meeting."

blocks.label: LLM output
blocks.content: "Hi Anna — thanks for the invite. I won't be able to make tomorrow, but I'd love to find another time next week. — Mike"

example.takeaway: Every word above was generated one token at a time, predicting what's most likely to come next.

slides.title: Token
slides.subtitle: Tokens / Context Window
description: The smallest unit an LLM works with. Not a word, not a letter — something in between. A token is roughly ¾ of an English word. Non-English words and accented characters (é, ñ, ü, ß) often use more tokens.

An LLM has a "context window" — the maximum number of tokens it can process at once, including both input and output. Everything an agent reads and writes in one conversation has to fit inside this window.

callout.label: Common confusion
callout.title: "Token = word"
callout.body: The word "Marmalade" can be 3 tokens. "Antidisestablishmentarianism" can be 8+. Tokens affect both cost and how much information an agent can handle at a time.

example.scenario: Watch a sentence get sliced into tokens.

blocks.label: Sentence
blocks.content: "AI is changing how we work."

blocks.label: Tokens (7)
blocks.content: [AI] [ is] [ changing] [ how] [ we] [ work] [.]

blocks.label: Tricky word
blocks.content: "Antidisestablishmentarianism" alone is 8+ tokens.

example.takeaway: Tokens decide both cost and how much an agent can hold in mind at once.

paragraphs: The hidden instruction that defines the agent's role, rules, and context. Users normally don't see it.
The system prompt is like a job description: it tells the agent who it is, what it's allowed to do, and how it should behave. It sets the frame for every response.
Example: "You are a legal assistant for a corporate law firm. Always answer in clear English. Never cite legislation you aren't certain about."
What the user actually writes — the question, task, or instruction sent to the agent in the conversation.
The user prompt is the visible input. The more context, examples, and structure the user provides, the better the response. A good prompt is more than a question — it's a detailed task description.
Example: "Review this contract and list the 5 most important risks for us as the supplier. Format the answer as a table."

bottomBar.text: Common misconception: "Prompt = just a question" — A prompt is instruction + context + examples + rules. The quality of the prompt determines the quality of the answer.

example.scenario: Same task, two prompts. Watch the answer change.

blocks.label: Weak prompt
blocks.content: "Write something about coffee."

blocks.label: Strong prompt
blocks.content: "Write a 3-sentence intro for a beginner blog post on home espresso. Casual tone, no jargon, end with a question."

example.takeaway: More context → more useful answer. Treat prompts like briefs, not questions.

slides.title: Hallucination
slides.subtitle: Confabulation / Fabrication
description: When an LLM generates text that sounds confident and convincing but is factually wrong. The model doesn't "know" it's wrong — it produces the most likely tokens with no concept of truth.
Hallucinations are an inherent property of LLMs, not a bug that can be fully removed. That's why RAG, source verification, and human oversight are critical in professional settings.

callout.label: Common misconception
callout.title: "AI lies on purpose"
callout.body: AI has no intent to deceive. Hallucinations occur because the model generates probable text, not verified facts. It can invent sources, numbers, and references that have never existed.

example.scenario: Confident, plausible — and completely made up.

blocks.label: Question
blocks.content: "Who wrote the 1987 paper on quantum tunneling in plant cells?"

blocks.label: LLM answer
blocks.content: "Dr. Sarah Whitfield, published in Nature Plant Biology, vol. 14, 1987."

blocks.label: Reality
blocks.content: No such person, paper, or journal exists.

example.takeaway: Always verify facts that matter. The model isn't lying — it's pattern-matching.

slides.heading: The agent in practice
slides.description: How an LLM becomes an autonomous worker that can act, retrieve knowledge, and solve tasks.

slides.title: Agent
slides.subtitle: Autonomous AI Agent
description: An AI agent is software that can plan, reason, and act independently to solve complex tasks. It uses an LLM as its brain, but it can also call tools, retrieve data, and adapt its approach as it goes.

The difference between a chatbot and an agent: a chatbot answers questions. An agent takes ownership of a task — it makes a plan, executes actions, and evaluates the result — like a skilled employee working independently.

callout.label: Common misconception
callout.title: "A chatbot is an agent"
callout.body: A chatbot reacts passively to input. An agent acts proactively — it plans, uses tools, accesses data, and completes tasks end-to-end without constant guidance.

example.scenario: Task: "Book me a hotel in Berlin under €150 for next Friday."

blocks.label: 1. Plan
blocks.content: Check calendar for travel dates → set price and location filters.

blocks.label: 2. Act
blocks.content: Search hotel API → filter results → check reviews → shortlist 3.

blocks.label: 3. Deliver
blocks.content: Present options → confirm choice → book and email confirmation.

example.takeaway: An agent owns the whole task — plan, execute, and deliver — not just one reply.

slides.title: RAG
slides.subtitle: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
description: A technique that gives an LLM access to external knowledge by searching documents, databases, or files — and inserting the relevant excerpts directly into the prompt.
Instead of relying on training data alone, the agent "looks up" your actual data before answering. RAG dramatically reduces hallucinations because the response is grounded in real information.

callout.label: Common misconception
callout.title: "RAG = just a search"
callout.body: RAG is more than search. It finds relevant text excerpts, ranks them, and weaves them into the prompt so the LLM can answer with context it was never trained on — like your internal contracts or project data.

example.scenario: User asks: "What's our refund policy on damaged goods?"

blocks.label: 1. Retrieve
blocks.content: Search company docs → find policy.pdf, section 4.2 on damaged goods.

blocks.label: 2. Augment
blocks.content: Insert that excerpt directly into the LLM's prompt as context.

blocks.label: 3. Generate
blocks.content: LLM answers using the actual policy — not its training data.

example.takeaway: Grounded in your real documents. Far less likely to hallucinate.

slides.heading: Tools & connections
slides.description: What an agent can do, and how it reaches into the outside world.

slides.title: Tool
slides.subtitle: Function / External Action
description: A tool is a specific action an agent can perform — like searching the web, reading a file, sending an email, or querying a database. Tools extend the agent's capabilities beyond pure text generation.
Without tools, an LLM can only write text. With tools, an agent can actually do things in the real world — fetch data, update systems, send messages, and much more.

tools.name: web_search
tools.desc: Searches the internet and returns relevant results.

tools.name: read_file
tools.desc: Reads the contents of a file from a connected system.

tools.name: send_email
tools.desc: Sends an email through the connected mail account.

example.scenario: Task: "What's the weather in Paris right now?"

blocks.label: Agent reasons
blocks.content: I need live data — my training won't help. Use the weather tool.

blocks.label: Tool call
blocks.content: get_weather(city: "Paris")

blocks.label: Result
blocks.content: { temp: 14°C, conditions: "light rain" } → "It's 14°C and raining lightly in Paris."

example.takeaway: Tools turn an LLM from a writer into a doer.

slides.title: Skills
slides.subtitle: Skill = Tool + Knowledge + Rules
description: A skill is a package that combines tools with domain knowledge and rules. Where a tool is a single action (e.g. "search a database"), a skill is a whole capability (e.g. "run a competitor analysis").
Skills make an agent specialized. They contain knowledge of when and how tools should be used in a given context — like an employee who has both the tools and the expertise to use them well.

callout.label: Skill examples
items: Weekly project report from Jira + Slack

blocks.label: Tools
blocks.content: web_search + scrape_page + summarize

blocks.label: Knowledge
blocks.content: Which sites to check, which data points matter (pricing, positioning, recent launches).

blocks.label: Rules
blocks.content: Always cite sources. Flag any claim older than 6 months. Output as a 1-page brief.

example.takeaway: A skill is the difference between "can search" and "can analyze".

slides.title: MCP
slides.subtitle: Model Context Protocol
description: MCP is an open standard that defines how AI agents connect to external tools and data sources. Think of it as USB for AI — a universal connector.
Before MCP, every integration had to be built from scratch. With MCP, an agent can connect to any compatible system through a standardized protocol — without custom code for each individual tool.

callout.label: Common confusion
callout.title: "MCP = an API"
callout.body: An API is the connection to one system. MCP is the protocol that standardizes the connection to many APIs. MCP lets agents reach hundreds of tools through one shared language instead of building individual integrations.

blocks.label: Without MCP
blocks.content: Build a custom integration for Slack. Another for Drive. Another for GitHub. Another for Jira. Maintain them all.

blocks.label: With MCP
blocks.content: Each system exposes an MCP server. The agent speaks one shared language to all of them.

example.takeaway: Like USB: plug it in once and it just works — across hundreds of tools.

slides.heading: Access points
slides.description: How users and systems reach the AI agent — the two interfaces.

slides.title: API
slides.subtitle: Application Programming Interface
description: An API is a set of rules that lets different programs talk to each other. Think of it like a menu at a restaurant — you don't need to know how the kitchen works, you just order from the menu.
The API defines what "dishes" (functions) are available, what you have to send (input), and what you get back (output). It's the standardized way software exchanges data.

callout.label: Common misconception
callout.title: "APIs are only for developers"
callout.body: You use APIs every day without realizing it. When you log in with Google, pay with Apple Pay, or check the weather in an app — apps are talking to each other through APIs behind the scenes.

example.scenario: Your weather app asks weather.com for the current conditions.

blocks.label: Request
blocks.content: GET https://api.weather.com/current?city=Paris

blocks.label: Response
blocks.content: { "temperature": 14, "condition": "rainy" }

blocks.label: Result
blocks.content: App displays "14°C — rainy" to the user.

example.takeaway: APIs let programs ask each other questions in a structured way.

slides.title: CLI
slides.subtitle: Command Line Interface
description: A text-based interface where you type commands instead of clicking. The CLI gives direct control over programs and is the foundation of automation.
While a graphical interface (GUI) is designed to be visual, a CLI is designed to be efficient. You can chain commands together, write scripts, and automate repetitive work in just a few lines.

callout.label: Common misconception
callout.title: "CLI is outdated"
callout.body: CLI is faster and easier to automate than GUI.

example.scenario: Save and commit a code change.

blocks.label: GUI
blocks.content: Open Source Control panel → review files → type a message → click Stage → click Commit. (4 steps, lots of mouse.)

blocks.label: CLI
blocks.content: git commit -am "Fix login bug"

example.takeaway: Less clicking, more doing — and trivially scriptable.

slides.heading: How it all fits together
slides.summary: The user sends a prompt via CLI or API → the agent (LLM + system prompt) retrieves relevant data via RAG → selects skills → calls tools through MCP → delivers the result back.

rows.right: LLM is one type of AI. AI is the umbrella.
Hallucination is probable text, not deliberate lying.
An agent acts independently with tools and planning.
Tool = one action. Skill = bundle of knowledge + tools.
MCP standardizes the connection to many APIs.
CLI is faster and easier to automate than GUI.
